title: Lesson 410
transcript: An important part of business continuity management and more specifically creating your BCP and especially your DRP plans is thinking about what are your external dependencies. In other words, As part of your plans on how you're going to recover after some disaster, you have to think about who do you rely upon? Which vendors, suppliers, contractors, etc. What are your external dependencies? And how are you going to make sure that those providers, vendors, whomever, are going to actually do what you need them to do in the event of a disaster? Let me give you a really good example. Let's say part of your disaster recovery plan is that you have, in order to restore the power to your data center, you have backup generators. and those backup generators burn diesel fuel, so you have diesel tanks on site. And one of your external dependencies related to diesel generators and diesel tanks is your fuel provider, the company that comes and delivers more fuel. Because let's say as part of planning for disaster, like maybe your data center is in Texas or Florida where they get hit by hurricanes. If you get hit by some massive hurricane, it's not uncommon that the power might be out for, say, a week. And you need to plan for this, because your fuel tank might only provide 24 hours worth of diesel fuel for your generator. So your external dependency, right? Like if you need to be able to keep your data center running off of your backup generator for seven days, you're gonna need to get seven fuel deliveries, right? One every single day. So this is your external dependency. You're relying upon this fuel supplier to come and actually deliver fuel to fill up your diesel tank to keep your data center running while the power is out after a hurricane is hit. And the reason I brought up this example is that not too many years ago, when Texas got hit by a massive hurricane, There was a number of companies that ran into this exact situation where they were running their facilities off of backup power. They had contracts in place with fuels providers to come and deliver more fuel to keep them up and running. But the fuel providers didn't deliver the fuel. And the reason the fuel providers didn't provide the fuel is because the state came in and said, no, you know, the most important thing for us in this disaster is to keep the hospitals up and running, the police stations up and running, the fire, the emergency services up and running. And so there was basically a declaration that said any of the sort of fuel suppliers, you're going to be doesn't matter what contracts you have in place, you're delivering fuel to these government facility, you know, to these hospitals and things like that to keep them up and running. And so these companies, despite the fact that they had contracts in place, it didn't matter. That fuel had been absconded with and put to better use probably. So I'm aware of one client I worked with in Texas that after this occurred, What they did for any future hurricanes is when they got service contracts in place to get fuel delivered, they actually went out of state. So they went to a fuel provider out of Georgia. And that way, if Texas gets hit by a hurricane, and the state says, nope, you know, all the fuel is going to emergency services. Well, the company could still get their fuel from an out of state provider, right? Because Texas might say, OK, all the fuel is going to these emergency services, but you know, if it's fuel coming from Georgia, that can still be much more expensive trucking it all the way in. But at least the data center will still be running. And so you have to have to think about this. So this is the whole idea of external dependencies. Think about what external dependencies you have for fuel, for water, cooling water, for power, for network connectivity, et cetera. And make sure you've clearly understood what these dependencies are. I mean, here's what you need to do, right? You must assess these external dependencies and have strategies in place to mitigate potential disruptions. Like for example, getting your fuel from out of state. So that's the idea of external dependencies and why it's important to analyze, think about these and put mitigating controls in place.
